title: Masala chai
subtitle: Spiced Indian tea simmered with ginger, cardamom, and milk.
excerpt: Black tea simmered with fresh ginger, cardamom, cinnamon, and whole milk. Sweetened with honey or sugar and strained into cups. Stronger and more aromatic than tea made with a teabag and a splash of milk.
sourceNotes: Masala chai is a spiced milky tea preparation from the Indian subcontinent, where it is made and sold by chai wallahs on every street corner and station platform. The word 'chai' simply means tea in Hindi; 'masala' means spice blend. The specific spice combination varies by region: cardamom and ginger appear throughout, while cloves, black pepper, and cinnamon vary by household and area. Masala chai became widely known in Britain through Indian restaurants and has since entered British domestic cooking.

content.text: The key difference between masala chai and a cup of tea with milk is that the tea simmers with the spices and milk together, rather than being brewed separately and mixed. This way the flavours integrate rather than sit on top of each other.

Fresh ginger is essential here. Ground ginger produces a harsher, dryer heat; fresh ginger produces warmth and a gentle fragrance. Bash the ginger lightly with the flat of a knife before slicing to release more flavour.

items.prepNote: black tea, English Breakfast or Assam

content.text: A small saucepan and a fine-mesh sieve or tea strainer.

Put {{water}} of water, the sliced ginger, crushed cardamom pods, cinnamon stick, and black pepper in a small saucepan. Bring to a boil, then reduce the heat and simmer for 3 minutes to infuse the spices.
Add the teabags and {{whole-milk}} of milk. Bring back to a gentle simmer and cook for 3–4 minutes, stirring occasionally. Do not let it boil hard or the milk will scorch.
Remove the teabags. Stir in {{honey}} of honey. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve directly into cups and serve hot.

items.cause: Teabags left in too long or simmered at too high a temperature.
items.fix: Remove the teabags after 3 minutes of simmering. Black tea becomes bitter quickly when boiled.

items.cause: Spices not bruised or steeped long enough.
items.fix: Crush the cardamom pods firmly before adding. Simmer the spices in water alone for 5 minutes before adding the milk.

items.symptom: Milk has scorched on the bottom
items.cause: Heat too high during the milk-simmering stage.
items.fix: Keep the heat at a gentle simmer and stir frequently. A burnt note cannot be removed.

items.symptom: Too sweet or not sweet enough
items.cause: Honey quantity is a matter of taste.
items.fix: Start with one tablespoon, taste, and add more. Adjust next time based on preference.

content.text: Chai in India is not a recipe but a practice: a constant, variable preparation that differs by city, by household, and by the individual who makes it. The version that arrived in British culture is more standardised than any Indian household version, partly because it came through restaurants and partly because British taste preferences shaped what stuck. The homemade version here is closer to a North Indian street chai than to the commercial 'chai latte' served in coffee shops, which typically uses a pre-made syrup and steamed milk.
